stage.name: Blockchain Foundations
stage.description: University-level introduction to blockchain architecture, consensus mechanisms, cryptographic primitives, and distributed systems theory.

generatedAgentConfigs.name: Prof. Chen
generatedAgentConfigs.persona: A rigorous computer science professor who explains distributed systems with precision and real-world data.

elements.content: What Is a Blockchain?
A cryptographically linked, append-only distributed ledger

7 tx/s
Bitcoin

~600GB
Chain size

Formal Definition

  • Hash-linked list of blocks B₀, B₁, …, Bₙ
  • Each block: Merkle root + H(prev) + nonce/sig + timestamp
  • Altering block Bᵢ invalidates every block after it
  • Attack success: (q/p)^z — drops exponentially with depth

Decentralization

  • ~17,000 reachable Bitcoin nodes (2024)
  • ~9,000 Ethereum nodes — no single point of failure
  • Open participation: anyone can run a full node
  • Censorship resistance via distributed validation

Throughput Reality

  • Bitcoin: 7 tx/s — intentionally conservative
  • Ethereum L1: ~15 tx/s base layer
  • Visa: ~24,000 tx/s — the gap drives L2 research
  • Bitcoin blockchain ≈ 600 GB total (April 2026)

Why It Matters

  • No trusted third party required (trustless settlement)
  • Transparent: every tx verifiable by anyone
  • Immutable: settled txs cannot be reversed
  • 24/7/365 — no banking hours, no holidays
Cryptographic Hash Functions
SHA-256: the foundation of Bitcoin's security

256-bit
Output

700 EH/s
Hash rate

Five Formal Properties

  • Pre-image resistance: Given H(x), finding x requires 2²⁵⁶ ops
  • Second pre-image: Can't find x' ≠ x with H(x')=H(x)
  • Collision resistance: Birthday paradox needs ~2¹²⁸ attempts
  • Avalanche effect: 1-bit input flip → ~50% output bits change
  • Deterministic: Same input always → same 256-bit output

Bitcoin SHA-256 in Practice

  • Block header hashed TWICE (SHA-256d) — prevents length-extension attacks
  • Mining target: hash must be below difficulty threshold T
  • Current difficulty: ~88 trillion (April 2026)
  • Expected hashes per block: D × 2³² ≈ 8.8 × 10²² attempts
  • Global hash rate: ~700 EH/s = 7×10²⁰ hashes/second

Why This Secures Bitcoin

  • Changing any tx changes Merkle root → changes block hash
  • Changes block hash → breaks next block's prev-hash link
  • Attacker must redo ALL subsequent Proof-of-Work
  • Cost to attack 51%: billions in hardware + electricity
  • SHA-256 has no known collision — mathematically sound
Merkle Trees — Efficient Tx Verification
Binary hash trees enable SPV without downloading the full chain

O(log n)
Proof size

320B
vs 32KB

Tree Structure

  • Leaf nodes: H(Tx₁), H(Tx₂), … H(Txₙ)
  • Parent node: H(left_child || right_child)
  • Root = single 32-byte fingerprint of ALL transactions
  • Bitcoin block header stores only the Merkle root (not all txs)
  • Odd number of leaves: last leaf duplicated

SPV Proof (Light Clients)

  • Simple Payment Verification — verify tx without full node
  • Need only: block header + Merkle proof path (log₂n hashes)
  • 1,000 tx block → only 10 hashes needed to prove inclusion
  • Reduces verification data: 32 KB → 320 bytes
  • Used by mobile wallets (Electrum, Ledger Live)

Why Merkle Trees Matter

  • Tamper-evident: changing any tx changes the root
  • Efficient: O(log n) proof vs O(n) full block download
  • Scalable: same root regardless of block size
  • Enables light nodes → billions of mobile users
  • Also used in: git, ZK-proofs, certificate transparency
Consensus: Proof-of-Work vs Proof-of-Stake
Two approaches to achieving Byzantine agreement at scale

140 TWh
PoW energy

-99.95%
PoS reduction

⛏ Proof-of-Work

  • Miners compete: find nonce so H(header) < target
  • Difficulty adjusts every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks) in Bitcoin
  • 51% attack cost: >$20B hardware + ~$15M/day electricity
  • Energy: ~140 TWh/year (≈ Netherlands) — intentional cost
  • Finality: probabilistic — 6 confirmations ≈ irreversible

🔒 Proof-of-Stake

  • Validators lock (stake) ETH as collateral — slash on misbehavior
  • Ethereum: 32 ETH minimum stake per validator
  • ~1M active validators post-Merge (Dec 2022)
  • Energy reduction: -99.95% vs PoW — 0.01 TWh/year
  • Finality: 2-epoch (~12.8 min) — cryptographically final

Comparison & Trade-offs

  • PoW: battle-tested 15 years, proven Sybil resistance
  • PoS: capital-efficient, faster finality, eco-friendly
  • PoW attack: need hash rate. PoS attack: need 33% stake
  • PoW: ASIC centralization risk. PoS: rich-get-richer risk
  • Both achieve Byzantine agreement for n > 3f+1 nodes
Byzantine Fault Tolerance
Lamport, Shostak & Pease (1982) — the math behind trustless consensus

3f+1
Min nodes

33%
Max traitors

The Core Theorem

  • System of n nodes can tolerate f Byzantine (malicious) faults
  • Required: n ≥ 3f + 1 — proven mathematically in 1982
  • Example: 100 nodes → tolerate up to 33 traitors
  • Below threshold: no algorithm can guarantee consensus
  • Bitcoin: PoW makes Byzantine attack economically infeasible

Types of Failures

  • Crash failure: node stops responding — easiest to handle
  • Byzantine failure: node sends arbitrary/conflicting data
  • Selfish mining: withold valid blocks to gain advantage
  • Double-spend attack: broadcast conflicting txs to splits
  • BFT assumes worst case: actively adversarial nodes

Modern BFT Implementations

  • PBFT (Practical BFT): O(n²) messages — used in permissioned chains
  • Tendermint: BFT + PoS — used by Cosmos, ~2s finality
  • HotStuff: linear BFT — basis of Diem, Aptos consensus
  • RPCA (Ripple): Federated BFT — no mining, 3-5s finality
  • Ethereum Casper: BFT-style finality layered on PoS
The Blockchain Trilemma
Security, Scalability, Decentralization — pick any two

7 tx/s
Bitcoin

65k tx/s
Solana

The Three Corners

  • Security: resistant to 51% attacks, Sybil attacks, double-spends
  • Scalability: high throughput (tx/s), low latency, cheap fees
  • Decentralization: many nodes, no central authority, open access
  • Coined by Vitalik Buterin — trade-offs are mathematically real
  • No blockchain has solved all three simultaneously at base layer

Where Each Chain Falls

  • Bitcoin: Security + Decentralization → sacrifices throughput (7 tx/s)
  • Solana: Security + Scalability → 65k tx/s but ~2k validators
  • Ripple (XRPL): Speed + Security → trusted UNL (semi-centralized)
  • Ethereum L1: Decentralization + Security → slow, expensive
  • BSC: fast and cheap → only 21 validators (centralized)

Solving It with L2s

  • Layer 2 moves computation off-chain, posts proofs on-chain
  • Optimistic Rollups: 2k+ tx/s, 7-day fraud proof window
  • ZK-Rollups: validity proof on-chain, instant finality
  • EIP-4844 (March 2024): blobs cut L2 fees 90%+ on Ethereum
  • XRPL sidechains: EVM-compatible, anchored to XRPL security
Smart Contracts & the EVM
Self-executing code that eliminated the need for intermediaries

$120B+
TVL locked

21k gas
Transfer

What Smart Contracts Are

  • Code stored on-chain, executed by every node independently
  • Trigger: specific conditions met → automatic execution
  • Immutable once deployed — no company can change the rules
  • Examples: DeFi protocols, NFT minting, DAOs, token vesting
  • $120B+ TVL locked in smart contracts (April 2026)

Ethereum Virtual Machine

  • EVM: stack-based, Turing-complete 256-bit VM
  • Every ETH node runs identical EVM state — global computer
  • Gas: computational cost unit — prevents infinite loops
  • 1 ETH block gas limit ≈ 30M gas → ~200 simple transfers
  • Solidity/Vyper compile to EVM bytecode — 140+ opcodes

Gas Economics & Limits

  • EIP-1559: base fee (burned) + priority tip (to validators)
  • Base fee adjusts per block — rises when blocks >50% full
  • Gas price = base_fee + tip. Peak: 500+ gwei (NFT craze 2021)
  • Complex DeFi tx: 200k–500k gas. Simple transfer: 21k gas
  • EVM security: reentrancy, overflow — audit is mandatory

content.html:
SHA-256 Avalanche Effect Demo

Type any message below. Watch how a 1-character change flips ~50% of the hash output.

Your input
SHA-256 hash (simulated)
--
Input + 1 character added
SHA-256 of modified input
--
Bits changed (avalanche effect)
--
Note: This uses a deterministic pseudorandom function to simulate SHA-256 behavior for educational purposes. Real SHA-256 is computed server-side.

actions.text: The avalanche effect is the most important property of a cryptographic hash function. Try it yourself — change one character and watch how roughly half the output bits flip. This is why hash functions are used for tamper detection: any modification is immediately apparent.

questions.question: A Bitcoin block contains 2,048 transactions. How many hashes are needed to prove a single transaction's inclusion using a Merkle proof?
questions.analysis: log₂(2048) = 11. A Merkle proof requires only the sibling hash at each level of the tree — 11 hashes instead of 2,048.

questions.question: The Byzantine Generals Problem states that consensus is achievable with N generals and f traitors only when:
questions.analysis: The Lamport-Shostak-Pease theorem: you need at least 3f+1 honest generals to achieve consensus with f Byzantine (malicious) generals. With fewer, traitors can prevent agreement.

questions.question: Which of the following are properties of a cryptographic hash function? (Select all that apply)
questions.analysis: Cryptographic hash functions must be pre-image resistant, collision resistant, and exhibit the avalanche effect. They are NOT reversible — that is the entire point of one-way functions.

questions.question: Ethereum's EIP-1559 introduced a base fee mechanism. What happens to this base fee?

options.label: It goes to validators as a reward
It is sent to the Ethereum Foundation
It funds the block producer's staking pool

questions.analysis: EIP-1559 burns the base fee, permanently removing ETH from circulation. Since August 2021, over 3.5 million ETH has been burned, creating deflationary pressure during periods of high network demand.

questions.question: In the Blockchain Trilemma, Bitcoin maximizes which two properties?
questions.analysis: Bitcoin prioritizes Security and Decentralization at the cost of scalability (7 tx/s). Every full node validates all transactions, requiring no trust, but limiting throughput.

questions.question: Explain why a selfish mining attack becomes viable when a mining pool exceeds approximately 25-33% of total hash rate, and what real-world consequence this has for Bitcoin's security model.
questions.commentPrompt: Award full marks for: (1) explaining that the pool withholds blocks to build a private chain, (2) releasing it at strategic moments to orphan honest blocks and claim more rewards than their hash share deserves, (3) noting this undermines the security assumption that honest majority = safe, even without a 51% majority.
